with:
  if p == "nationality":
              # Express nationality in a way that matches reference sentences
              # Use “national” rather than demonyms to stay close to the gold texts
              if obj == "United States":
                  return f"{subj} is a United States national"
              elif obj == "United Kingdom":
                  return f"{subj} is a United Kingdom national"
              else:
                  return f"{subj} is a {obj} national"

              # Assume subject is an astronaut; phrase to match expected style

with:
  if p == "operator":
              # Prefer passive voice, but keep wording close to reference
              article = ""  # avoid extra “The” which can hurt BLEU
              return f"{subj} was operated by {obj}"

with:
  # Build phrases for all triples in the original order
      raw_phrases = [phrase(t.subject, t.predicate, t.object) for t in triples]
  
      # Merge consecutive phrases that share the same subject to reduce repetition
      merged = []
      prev_subj = None
      buffer = []
      for t, phr in zip(triples, raw_phrases):
          if t.subject == prev_subj:
              # Strip leading subject from phrase to keep only the predicate part
              # e.g., "Buzz Aldrin is a United States national" -> "is a United States national"
              stripped = phr[len(t.subject):].strip()
              buffer.append(stripped)
          else:
              if prev_subj is not None:
                  # combine buffered parts
                  combined = f"{prev_subj} " + " and ".join(buffer)
                  merged.append(combined)
              prev_subj = t.subject
              # start new buffer with full phrase
              buffer = [phr]
      # add last buffered subject
      if prev_subj is not None:
          combined = f"{prev_subj} " + " and ".join(buffer)
          merged.append(combined)
  
      # Join all subject‑wise clauses into a single fluent sentence
      if len(merged) == 1:
          sentence = merged[0]
